title: Revoke and remove committed GCP service account private key
immediate: Step 1: Go to Google Cloud Console -> IAM & Admin -> Service Accounts -> find paper-inventory@paper-inventory-488411.iam.gserviceaccount.com -> Keys tab -> delete key ID 4f90bf2f1afc. This revokes the leaked private key. Step 2: Create a new key and store it OUTSIDE the repository directory. Step 3: Update GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS in .env to point to the new location. Step 4: Add the old filename to .gitignore immediately.
command: git rm --cached paper-inventory-488411-4f90bf2f1afc.json && echo 'paper-inventory-*.json' >> .gitignore && git commit -m 'Remove committed GCP service account key'
prevention: Store GCP key files in a user-level directory outside any repo (e.g. ~/.gcp/). Never store service account JSON inside a project directory.

immediate: Rotate remaining live keys: Anthropic Console (ANTHROPIC_API_KEY starts sk-ant-api03-toxj) and Perplexity (PERPLEXITY_API_KEY starts pplx-fXEtZZ11). After rotating, run git rm --cached .env and create a .env.example with placeholder values.
command:
git rm --cached .env && echo 'GEMINI_API_KEY=your_key_here
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=your_key_here
PERPLEXITY_API_KEY=your_key_here
GROQ_API_KEY=your_key_here' > .env.example && git add .env.example && git commit -m 'security: remove .env from tracking, add .env.example'
prevention: Install git-secrets or gitleaks as a pre-commit hook to prevent future accidental key commits. Add '.env' to .gitignore (it already is, but ensure git rm --cached is run). Use a secrets manager (1Password, Doppler, AWS Secrets Manager) for team environments.

immediate: Add a secret-based guard to the /api/admin/clear endpoint. At minimum, require a matching ADMIN_SECRET header checked against an environment variable. For a single-user local app, add an IP whitelist check (127.0.0.1 only) or simply remove the route entirely and rely on scripts/clear-db.ts for local development.
prevention: Never expose destructive admin operations as unauthenticated HTTP endpoints, even for local-only apps. Use environment-variable-gated middleware or remove the route and use CLI scripts instead.

immediate: The OCR worker needs a reliable production path. The most robust solution for Next.js is to copy the compiled worker to the public/ directory during the build step, or to use a custom webpack configuration to emit the worker as a standalone file. Alternatively, use ts-node in production or switch to a Next.js-compatible worker bundling approach.
command:
# Add to package.json scripts:
# "postbuild": "mkdir -p .next/server/chunks/workers && cp src/workers/ocr.worker.ts .next/server/chunks/workers/"
# Or use tsx in production: change workerPath logic to always use tsx with ts-node for the worker
prevention: Worker thread files in Next.js projects need explicit bundling strategy. Document the production worker deployment process and add a build-time check that validates the worker file exists before deployment completes.

immediate: Fix the three failing tests by updating the source code to match the intended architecture: (1) Change 'if (item.status === 'queued')' to 'if (!item.rawOcr)' in queue/manager.ts. (2) Replace the Promise.all in index.ts with sequential awaits for triage and grounding. (3) Remove 'claude-sonnet' from all DEEP_DIVE_FALLBACKS arrays in index.ts.
prevention: Run 'npm test' as part of every development session. The failing tests are architecture regression guards that prevent known bad patterns from being reintroduced.

immediate: Same fix as ISSUE-004 -- the source code needs to be brought into conformance with the documented architecture. The three specific changes are: OCR gate condition, sequential pipeline execution, and claude-sonnet removal from fallbacks.
command: npm test  # Run after applying fixes to verify all 5 tests pass
prevention: Add 'npm test' to the git pre-commit hook so architecture regressions are caught before commit.

immediate: Delete src/lib/ai/openai.ts (the deprecated Vercel AI SDK file) and remove the unused packages from package.json. Verify no other files import from this path before deletion.
command:
# 1. Grep to verify no other imports:
# grep -r 'from.*lib/ai/openai' src/ (should only find openai.ts itself)
# 2. Delete the file: rm src/lib/ai/openai.ts
# 3. Uninstall unused deps:
# npm uninstall ai @ai-sdk/anthropic @ai-sdk/openai
prevention: When adding a new provider adapter, immediately remove or replace the old one. Maintain a single, clear import path for each AI provider to avoid divergence.

immediate: Consolidate the database schema to a single source of truth. Either (a) make schema.sql the runtime source and read it with fs.readFileSync in db/index.ts, or (b) remove schema.sql and keep only the inlined version in db/index.ts. Update the Item interface to include all fields present in the runtime schema (especially lockedFields, valuation, analysis_history).
command:
# Option B (keep inlined schema): Delete schema.sql and add a code comment:
# rm src/lib/db/schema.sql
# Then update Item interface in items.ts to include lockedFields: string
prevention: Never maintain the same schema in more than one place. Use a migration library (drizzle-orm, Kysely) for future projects to enforce schema-as-code with proper version control.

immediate: Replace the boilerplate README with a project-specific one. At minimum include: (1) what paper-inventory does, (2) required environment variables, (3) setup instructions, (4) how to run in dev, (5) how to run the worker, (6) how to run tests. A proper README.md has been generated at the project root as part of this X-Ray analysis.
command: # README.md has been generated. Review and adjust to match current state.
prevention: Treat README as part of the PR checklist. Add a check: 'Does the README reflect the current state of the project?' for any significant feature addition.
